common_name: Black Knight Butterfly Bush (Dark Purple Buddleja)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; deadhead regularly to extend bloom season and, critically, to prevent seed formation — Buddleja davidii is considered invasive in parts of the Pacific Northwest and some mid-Atlantic states; remove all spent flower spikes before seeds mature.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Black Knight is the most popular dark-purple butterfly bush — a vigorous Buddleja davidii cultivar producing long (12–18 inch), conical, densely packed flower spikes in a rich, deep violet-purple to nearly black-purple color that is the darkest flower color readily available in any buddleja cultivar. The intense color contrasts dramatically with the silver-gray to gray-green lance-shaped foliage and creates an outstanding late-summer focal point. Like all butterfly bushes, Black Knight is extraordinarily attractive to butterflies, particularly swallowtails, monarchs, fritillaries, and skippers — which visit in such numbers during bloom that a well-established Black Knight shrub becomes a butterfly feeding station rivaling any other garden plant. Bloom runs from July through September (and often through October in zone 9) with regular deadheading. In zone 9, butterfly bush is a woody perennial that dies back significantly in winter and regrows vigorously from the woody base in spring. Cut back hard in February — to 12 inches from the ground — to stimulate vigorous new growth and maximum flower production. Note: Buddleja davidii is listed as invasive or potentially invasive in the Pacific Northwest and some mid-Atlantic regions; in the Gulf South it self-seeds less aggressively, but deadheading before seed set is still best practice. Sterile varieties (Buddleja 'Blue Chip,' 'Miss Ruby') are available for regions where invasive concerns apply.

common_name: Nanho Blue Butterfly Bush (Compact Blue-Lavender Buddleja)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Nanho Blue is a compact selection reaching 4–5 feet, smaller than standard butterfly bush — suitable for typical residential landscapes. Deadhead regularly to prevent seed formation.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; Nanho Blue is the most popular compact butterfly bush — a smaller-growing Buddleja davidii cultivar in the Nanho series reaching 4–5 feet tall and wide, compared to the 6–10 feet of standard-sized selections like Black Knight. The reduced size makes Nanho Blue appropriate for typical residential garden spaces where full-size butterfly bush would quickly overwhelm the surrounding plants. The flower color is clear lavender-blue to blue-violet — a cooler, more blue-toned color than many butterfly bush cultivars that trend toward red-violet and purple. The fine-textured, silver-gray foliage is particularly attractive in the Nanho series, with narrower, more delicate leaves than standard cultivars. Like all Buddleja davidii cultivars, Nanho Blue is extraordinarily attractive to butterflies — the honey-scented flowers attract admirals, swallowtails, and fritillaries through the summer. Deadhead regularly to extend bloom and prevent seed set. In zone 9, cut the entire plant back to 12–18 inches in February; vigorous new growth emerges quickly when temperatures warm. Butterfly bush is drought-tolerant once established and actually performs better in lean, somewhat dry soils — in rich, moist soil the growth is rank and floppy.

planting.note: Direct sow on the soil surface (light needed for germination — do not bury seeds) or plant container divisions in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Cheyenne Spirit can be grown from seed with good germination rates, producing first-year flowers in the spring-to-fall flowering window.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Cheyenne Spirit is the 2013 All-America Selections Flower Award winner — a seed-grown echinacea with outstanding, wide-range flower color variability that produces a multi-color meadow effect from a single seed packet. Individual plants flower in shades ranging from deep red, orange, yellow, cream, and white to coral, rose, and purple — a spectrum of colors that, when multiple plants are grown together, creates a dynamic, painterly mix reminiscent of a wildflower meadow. Unlike named clonal varieties with fixed colors, Cheyenne Spirit's color variability is part of its appeal, ensuring a naturalistic, mixed-color planting effect. The flowers have the classic Echinacea form — broad, flat petals surrounding a prominent, spiny, coppery-orange central cone — and are produced from June through October in zone 9. Cheyenne Spirit is one of the few echinacea cultivars that reliably flowers in the first growing season from seed, making it useful for quick establishment of pollinator gardens. The diverse color range means each planting is unique. Outstanding for meadow-style gardens, naturalistic borders, and any situation where a dynamic, multi-color late-summer to fall display is desired.

common_name: Lady in Red Salvia (Red Texas Sage, Scarlet Salvia coccinea)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 6 weeks early; in zone 9, Lady in Red often self-seeds so prolifically that it naturalizes as a perennial colony — once established, allow the self-seeded seedlings to fill in around the original planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Lady in Red is a compact, All-America Selections winning selection of Salvia coccinea — the native Texas sage or scarlet salvia — producing vivid, true scarlet-red tubular flowers on compact, well-branched plants from spring through frost in zone 9. Unlike the sprawling, leggy habit of unselected Salvia coccinea, Lady in Red stays compact and bushy at 18–24 inches with neat, square-stemmed, slightly aromatic foliage. The brilliant red tubular flowers are intensely attractive to hummingbirds, which hover around each bloom spike, and the plant is nearly always in active use by feeding hummingbirds during the long Gulf South summer. In zone 9, Lady in Red is treated as a short-lived perennial or vigorous annual that typically self-seeds so freely it naturalizes into a colony that perpetuates indefinitely without replanting. The naturalized seedlings are genetic variations that may range slightly from the named cultivar, but generally maintain the compact habit and red flower color of the parent. The salvia's ability to grow in partial shade as well as full sun makes it more versatile than most red-flowering summer annuals, fitting under light tree canopy and in dappled-light conditions. Outstanding for butterfly and hummingbird gardens.

planting.note: Plant container divisions in spring in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; lemon thyme requires excellent drainage and lean soil — in zone 9's summer heat and humidity, poorly drained soil causes crown rot. Raised beds and containers with gritty mix are ideal in zone 9.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, gritty soil in full sun; Lemon thyme is a hybrid thyme with an intensely pleasant lemon-citrus fragrance and flavor overlaid on the traditional thyme aromatic — the combination of thyme's herbal character with a bright, clean citrus note makes lemon thyme one of the most useful and distinctive culinary herbs for fish, poultry, tea, desserts, and anywhere a citrus-thyme combination is desired. The low, spreading, mat-forming habit (4–8 inches tall, spreading 12–18 inches) is elegant in rock gardens, between stepping stones, and as a fragrant ground cover in well-drained, sunny areas. In summer, small pale-pink to lavender flowers attract bees. The most commonly grown lemon thyme cultivar is 'Silver Queen' — a variegated selection with silver-margined green leaves that adds ornamental value to the culinary utility. 'Aureus' (Gold Lemon Thyme) has yellow-variegated leaves. The plain green form provides the most intense flavor. In zone 9's summer humidity, lemon thyme is the most heat-sensitive common thyme — it benefits from afternoon shade during peak summer heat and absolutely requires sharp drainage to survive zone 9 summers. Container culture in a porous mix with excellent drainage is often the most reliable approach in Gulf Coast gardens.
